title: Step 1: View the list of clusters.

sections.heading: Single-Region Cluster Backups
sections.body:
With single-region cluster backups, Atlas:

- Determines the order of nodes to try to snapshot using the following
  algorithm:

  - Snapshots on a secondary. 1 Then,

  - Snapshots the node with the lowest priority if possible. 2
    Then,

  - Snapshots incrementally from one snapshot to the next if possible.
    3 Then,

  - Snapshots node lexically first by hostname.

  1 If there is a tie, Atlas skips to the next step to determine the node to snapshot.

  2 If there is a tie, Atlas then favors the node that can be snapshotted incrementally from the previous snapshot (i.e., node using the same disk).

  3 If there is a tie, Atlas then favors the node with the lexicographically smallest hostname.

- Once the node order is determined, tries to snapshot a node. If a
  selected node is unhealthy, Atlas tries to snapshot the next
  node that it favors.

- Stores the snapshots in the same cloud region as the cluster.

- Retains snapshots based on your
  [base snapshot schedule](cloud-provider-retention-policy).

![Cloud Backup of the Primary](/backup/images/cloud-provider-snapshot-single-region-primary.svg)

![A Cloud Backup of the Secondary](/backup/images/cloud-provider-snapshot-single-region-secondary.svg)

Atlas automatically creates a new snapshot storage volume if the
existing snapshot storage volume becomes invalid. Atlas creates the
new volume in the same region as the cluster's current primary.
Atlas then takes a full-copy snapshot to maintain backup
availability and continues using that member and its corresponding
region for further incremental snapshots.

Events that can cause an election to select a new node for the snapshot
storage volume include:

- Changing the Atlas cluster tier,

- Decreasing the Atlas cluster's storage volume,

- Changing the Atlas cluster's region, and

- Maintenance performed by Atlas or the cluster's cloud provider.

> **See Also:** To learn more about snapshot retention, see
> [base snapshot schedule](cloud-provider-retention-policy).

sections.heading: Multi-Region Cluster Backups
sections.body:
With multi-region cluster backups, Atlas:

- Determines the order of nodes to snapshot using the following
  algorithm:

  - Snapshots in the highest priority region if possible. 1
    Then,

  - Snapshots on a secondary. 2 Then,

  - Snapshots the node with the lowest priority if possible. 3
    Then,

  - Snapshots incrementally from one snapshot to the next if possible.
    4 Then,

  - Snapshots node lexically first by hostname.

  1 If there is a tie, Atlas then compares based on the descending order of priority.

  2 If there is a tie, Atlas skips to the next step to determine the node to snapshot.

  3 If there is a tie, Atlas then favors the node that can be snapshotted incrementally from the previous snapshot (i.e., node using the same disk).

  4 If there is a tie, Atlas then favors the node with the lexicographically smallest hostname.

- Tries to snapshot a node once the node order is determined. If a
  selected node is unhealthy, Atlas tries to snapshot the next
  node that it favors.

- Retains snapshots based on your [base snapshot schedule](cloud-provider-retention-policy).

![Cloud Backup of the Primary](/backup/images/cloud-provider-snapshot-multi-region-primary.svg)

![A Cloud Backup of the Secondary](/backup/images/cloud-provider-snapshot-multi-region-secondary.svg)

Atlas automatically creates a new snapshot storage volume if the
existing snapshot storage volume becomes invalid. Atlas creates the
new volume in the same region as the cluster's current primary.
Atlas then takes a full-copy snapshot to maintain backup
availability and continues using that member and its corresponding
region for further incremental snapshots.

Events that can cause an election to select a new node for the snapshot
storage volume include:

- Changing the Atlas cluster tier,

- Decreasing the Atlas cluster's storage volume,

- Changing the Atlas cluster's highest priority region, and

- Maintenance performed by Atlas or the cluster's cloud provider.

> **See Also:** To learn more about snapshot retention, see
> [base snapshot schedule](cloud-provider-retention-policy).

sections.heading: Global Cluster Backups
sections.body:
Atlas can back up Global Clusters using
Cloud Backups as their backup method. Atlas restores the shards
in the source cluster to the corresponding shards in the target cluster
using the same order as specified in the cluster configuration.

> **Example:** `shard0` in the source cluster is restored to `shard0` in the
> target cluster.

> **Note:** If you used the API (Application Programming Interface) to create your Global Cluster, the zones are
> defined in the `replicationSpecs` parameter in the
> [Create One Cluster](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupcluster) and
> [Modify One Cluster](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updategroupcluster) API (Application Programming Interface) endpoints.

If the cluster configurations of the source and target clusters do not
match, shard data may migrate to a different cloud provider zone than
where it resided in the source cluster. After Atlas completes the
restore operation, the MongoDB [balancer](manual/reference/glossary) for the target cluster
migrates the data back to the zone where it resided in the source
cluster if your clusters meet the following requirements:

- Both clusters have enabled a Global Cluster on the same
  collection

- Both clusters use the same [shard keys](manual/reference/glossary) for the
  Global Writes-enabled collection

> **Note:** If the Global Writes-enabled collection on the target cluster does
> not contain any data, the MongoDB balancer for the cluster
> automatically distributes any data that you later add to the
> collection among the target cluster's shards.

To enable global writes on the target cluster:

sections.heading: Continuous Cloud Backups
sections.body:
Continuous Cloud Backups replay the [oplog](manual/reference/glossary) to restore a cluster from a
particular point in time within a window specified in the Backup
Policy.

You may opt to
[Configure Backup Options for the Cluster](create-cluster-backups).
Configure your continuous cloud backup window with the
[Backup Policy Editor](cps-backup-policies).

> **Note:** Enabling continuous cloud backups increases the monthly cost of your
> cluster.
>
> To learn more about the cost implications, see
> [billing](billing-backup-cloud-provider-snapshots).

Your cluster's snapshots stay within the cloud provider's storage
service under the cluster or shard's [highest priority region](deploy-across-multiple-regions). Oplog backups on AWS (Amazon Web Services) clusters
use standard AWS (Amazon Web Services) S3 (Simple Storage Service) encryption and Google Cloud clusters use standard [Google
Cloud Storage](https://cloud.google.com/storage) encryption.

In single-region and multi-region sharded clusters, Atlas creates
a separate backup for each shard in the shard's primary region.

> **Note:** 
>
> Clusters with continuous cloud backups enabled store [oplog](manual/reference/glossary) data
> in object storage according to your cloud provider:
>
> - S3 (Simple Storage Service) for AWS (Amazon Web Services)
>
> - Azure Blob Storage for Azure (Microsoft Azure)
>
> - Google Cloud Storage for Google Cloud

The following actions cause all existing oplog backups to be deleted.
All existing snapshots remain intact, but Atlas removes previously
preserved oplog data when:

- You disable continuous cloud backups for your cluster.

- The cluster receives an excessive number of writes. The cluster
  processes a large number of writes that causes the oplog to roll over
  before backup collects it.

  > **Example:** 
  >
  > - You sized your oplog for one hour of its usual write traffic,
  >   say 1,000 operations.
  >
  > - Database activity results in a large number of writes to the
  >   oplog, say 2,000 operations.
  >
  > - The number of writes result in the oplog dropping older
  >   records. This example would lose 1,000 operations.
  >
  > - Backup should collect operation #1, but it collects #1,001
  >   instead.

If you [Move a Cluster to a Different Region](move-cluster) your cluster's [highest priority region](deploy-across-multiple-regions) or if MongoDB
migrates oplog data to a different region:

- Atlas retains data in both the old and new regions until your
  continuous cloud backup window is represented in the new region. Once
  the continuous cloud backup window is represented in the new region,
  Atlas deletes the data in the old region.

- You will be billed for storage in both the old and new regions for
  the days following the region change. You must disable
  continuous cloud backup and reenable it to prevent billing in both regions.

  > **Note:** If you disable continuous cloud backup, Atlas will delete the
  > continuous cloud backup history.

When you use continuous cloud backups to restore a cluster from a previous
point in time, Atlas retains the cluster's [oplog](manual/reference/glossary). You
can use continuous cloud backups repeatedly to restore the cluster to any point
in its continuous cloud backup window **except** between when you initiated a
restore and when Atlas completes a snapshot after the restore.

sections.heading: Consistency and Snapshots
sections.body:
Atlas maintains [causal consistency](manual/core/read-isolation-consistency-recency) when it takes snapshots
except for the size statistics that [collStats](manual/reference/command/collStats) and
`db.[collection].count()` report. The size statistics  that [collStats](manual/reference/command/collStats) and
`db.[collection].count()` report might be inaccurate.

Atlas coordinates the time across all shards for sharded clusters to ensure
that snapshots include all documents written to every shard and node as of the scheduled
snapshot time.

sections.heading: View M10+ Backup Snapshots
sections.body:
**Atlascli:**

Atlas CLI

To list cloud backup snapshots for your project and cluster using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas backups snapshots list <clusterName> [options]

```

To return the details for the snapshot you specify using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas backups snapshots describe <snapshotId> [options]

```

To learn more about the syntax and parameters for the previous commands, see the Atlas CLI documentation for
[atlas backups snapshot list](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-backups-snapshots-list) and
[atlas backups snapshots describe](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-backups-snapshots-describe).

Related Links

- [Install the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/install-atlas-cli)

- [Connect to the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli)

**Atlas UI:**

Atlas UI

Atlas displays existing snapshots for a given cluster on the
Snapshots page. To view snapshots that Atlas has
already taken:

Alternatively, to view a list of all `M10+` clusters with
cloud backup enabled:

If you choose to retain backups from a cluster you
terminate, they remain accessible here under their source
cluster name until they expire or you delete them.

**Atlasapi:**

Atlas Administration API

The Atlas Administration API provides different endpoints for retrieving
replica set snapshots and sharded cluster snapshots.

- [Return One Snapshot for One Replica Set Cluster](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getgroupclusterbackupsnapshot)

- [Return All Snapshots for One Replica Set Cluster](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupclusterbackupsnapshots)

- [Return One Snapshot for One Sharded Cluster](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getgroupclusterbackupsnapshotshardedcluster)

- [Return All Snapshots for One Sharded Cluster](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupclusterbackupsnapshotshardedclusters)

> **Note:** 
>
> To monitor a backup restore job until it completes, you must have
> [Project Read Only](<Project Read Only>) access or higher to the specific project.
>
> To start a restore job, you must have [Project Backup Recovery Operator](<Project Backup Recovery Operator>), [Project Backup Manager](<Project Backup Manager>), or [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to the project.
>
> For cross-organization or cross-project restores, the required permissions
> apply to both the source and target projects. You must have the necessary
> permissions: [Project Backup Manager](<Project Backup Manager>) or [Project Owner](<Project Owner>)
> in both projects to initiate or manage such restore operations.
